summary: Mock behavioral interview for an Engineering Manager role at Uber. Josh interviews Ragu, who has 10+ years of EM experience at large companies. Topics covered: conflict resolution (Amazon RedShift Oracle-to-DynamoDB migration), a product the candidate is most proud of (ground-up accounting close platform), technical debt management, leadership style, accountability practices, and career trajectory. Includes detailed post-interview feedback on STAR format, leading with headlines, and articulating business impact.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. So, this is a mock interview for an engineering manager role at Uber. I'll be speaking with Ragu today about himself and who he is as an engineering manager, what's important to him, and one or two projects that he's led and how he approaches managing and leading other engineers. So, thanks for joining, Ragu. Really appreciate your time. Why don't you go ahead and tell me about yourself?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Hey Josh, nice to meet you. Thanks for having me. So yeah, I'm Ragu. I'm an engineering manager. I spent over 10 years as an engineering manager with big large companies. I have had the opportunity to, you know, start and like be a founder member for large multiple engineering teams. I've grown global teams across London and India and yeah I've been very successful at hiring and growing teams and landing a lot of impact through delivering large bets for the organizations I worked with.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Fantastic. Tell me about a time that you resolved a conflict.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay. So this was a time when we were doing this project at Amazon where there were multiple — or entire Amazon was actually involved in something called RedShift, which is basically deprecating Oracle databases and moving to Amazon's internal tech stack using either DynamoDB or the relational database service. And obviously all teams were frantically trying to get to their goals of being able to do this. Now, so one of the things which happened at the time was we realized we had to make changes to one of our services. We almost had to rewrite it because the way it was implemented was, if it was an Oracle database it was allowed to store some of the PII data which is the like the secure data, but if we have to move out we would have had to build another solution. Now the main conflict there was that we realized very quickly that that's not even something we should be doing, or we shouldn't even be storing that. We were not the source of truth for that data and that data should reside in some other system. Now obviously when we approached that team that owned that data or should be owning that data they were challenged with their own timelines and they did not have the bandwidth to make these changes. So we had a couple of meetings and we did not end up with any solution. So one of the ways which I thought at the time is that the engineer who owned this project from my team was on a career path to the next level or was kind of ready to go to the next level and needed an opportunity to kind of showcase or demonstrate those next level behaviors. So I kind of created a plan where we would do some of the initial work which would just set a platform for moving this data into the upstream team and we will just store the metadata about that particular data and create service endpoints for them to use later. We next went to them with this approach. They still would have had to do a little bit of work. It wasn't going to be much for them, which would kind of delay them in their own work, and that was the way forward to kind of solve this situation. The engineer did the work, you know, it was a bit of a win-win for everyone. The manager of the other team decided to give good feedback for the engineer on my team who got promoted and we all kind of were able to complete the project on time.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: That sounds like that was a difficult or challenging situation. What was your specific role in this situation?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: My specific role was working with the engineer from my team to come up with a solution for this problem. There was — or we had to make sure that we could not complete this project without moving out some of the PII data and if it had to go in and sit in some other team, the logical team or the logical owners for that data did not have the bandwidth. So I had to kind of mediate with the engineers and the engineering manager of that team along with the engineer on my team and come up with a solution that works for everyone. And then once we cross this obstacle and go live with the RedShift project, we would then work with the upstream team again to make sure we have a different milestone where we migrate off that service completely and they are able to kind of stand up their service which would own the data.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: What would you say was the impact of this project? Or maybe better said, what would happen if nothing were done? If you didn't lead through this?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I don't think that was an option. The other options were: stay with Oracle, which was not an option; continue storing the PII data, which was again not an option because DynamoDB does not allow — or at least back then did not allow — us to store that data securely; and the upstream team would have to build and launch a new service which was the right thing to do but we kind of just helped them to come up with a solution in the intermediate that would work, where we stored the metadata. We temporarily created a service that would store the data on their side and we were the only client for that period and then they would kind of expand it to other clients.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: So what was the impact to the business by achieving this project?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So the RedShift project was a project that came straight from the S-team at Amazon, which is like the very top senior leadership at Amazon. So it was again not negotiable in any way, either in terms of the timelines or the solution. So if we hadn't done this, there was no material benefit to any of the business teams across Amazon for people doing this project, but it had a much bigger impact with the fact that we would be saving probably hundreds of millions of dollars by using our own technology. So cost savings by using Amazon technology for storing this sensitive data. Got it, or at least disambiguating the sensitive data, it sounds like. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Cool. Let's take a turn towards a slightly different topic. What would you say is a product that you're most proud of?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Product that I'm most proud of. Okay. So I can tell about an accounting close platform that I developed. I was one of the first, and I'm proud of it for multiple reasons. A: this was a ground-up new product which became the central tool of choice for the company I was working in to complete all the financial close activity for that company. B: this was not only a ground-up new product. This was also a team that I developed — basically hired and grew as part of this team that I started. So I started a new team. I hired a lot of good engineers who went on to do very successful things for the product. I was also able to deprecate a lot of legacy systems. I was able to stand up this tool to become the single unified tool for performing financial flows automation. So, okay. So, it was a ground-up product.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: So what would you say was your — the highlight of that project for you? What made you the most proud?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: The fact that it was a big platform. There were four different modules in it and it was a huge product and at the same time it was very stable from its very go. We had only maybe 40 or 50 people who used the product at launch. It went on to have like more than 500 users within a year or two. And at the same time it was one of those rare products that never got any — you know what we call severe incidents or high priority tasks that kind of blocked users. It was extremely stable in terms of quality. There were — and that kind of led to a lot of adoption of the tool and we kept on adding a lot of big features and yet it always seemed to work scalably without going down or having any kind of product issues.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: That sounds interesting. Tell me about a decision or a trade-off you had to make in that project.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I think one of the decisions was, once we started getting a lot of adoption, what I realized is one of the modules had a backend engine which we realized is not going to scale. Now we had an option to go back to the users and say we need to kind of rebuild the internals of that engine to kind of make sure it scales for all the quick adoption that's happening — from like a few hundred cards we would be going to thousands of cards in less than six months. So one of the decisions I took was we stopped a new feature that we were developing to re-prioritize rebuilding this engine. And we did it in a way where we did not want the users to come back and do all these long UATs. We created a service that would compare the before and after in a very easy way for engineers to kind of just work with it. And we kind of created reporting that would go out and show that the new code did exactly what the old code would have done except that it is now far more scalable. So with that what happened is we were able to scale for the year end because the year end close is almost always the most critical for a company. And we went live with this rewriting of the engine and it scaled and we did not have any other problem. Now we did not have a new feature which came later in Q1 but that feature was not — would not have soured the experience of year end close as such.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, thank you. Let's go in another direction. Appreciate you sharing these stories. I think those are great — getting to know you a little better. Let's talk about technical debt. You know, as a product team, technical debt is always a trade-off. How do you — what are your views on technical debt generally speaking? And maybe even tell me about a time where you had to write down some technical debt — but I'm curious to hear your general thoughts about technical debt.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure. So I think I just gave an example where I think we kind of built a system that did not have technical debt, or which kind of did not ever lead to a situation with tech debt. So that's the highlight of my career in terms of building that kind of a product. But I would also say that that's because I had a lot of experience getting hurt with technical debt in the past. So there were a lot of lessons learned on, you know, what not to do, which I think I was successful in this new project. But going back to your question, Josh — so I had teams that I have kind of joined, or products that I have inherited, that had a lot of tech debt. And the things — or my observation is — we always tend to either talk too much into the future, that oh this is — if we need to kind of fix this we probably need to rebuild the whole thing, or we need to kind of have a two year engineering plan to kind of rewrite the product or something like that. But I can give some example where one of the teams that I was leading did have a lot of tech debt as such because it was written years ago when the team was much smaller, the product was much smaller, it wasn't processing the kind of billions of dollars that it was processing at that time. So there were a few tiny steps that I think I had my team take on a continuous basis. A: just improve the overall instrumentation in the code and start gathering metrics. So which means to say that, you know, do we even know what's working well and what's not? And if something is not working well, probably can we get more insights on a week-to-week basis. So one or two of the sprints I had my team purely work on adding more instrumentation and kind of generating the metrics that would go into our alerting and monitoring tool and start looking at this on a more constant per-week basis, because if I had to go to my leadership and talk about the weekly operational metrics we did not even have enough of that. So with that in mind, it was a steady progress made in terms of just collecting the metrics and reviewing them internally as a team every week when the on-call would kind of hand off the operations to the next engineer. But one of the things we started seeing is there were a lot of things — or a lot of misconceptions — we had about our own product. We used to have a website and we thought that's where everyone comes and creates these agreements. But what we realized is more than 70% of the agreements in our system are created by an upstream team calling our APIs. They never through the website. So editing an agreement is what was done mostly on our website, never much of creation. So we built all this kind of metrics continuously and it led to something much more impactful. It helped us to identify where even we should focus, what are the things that we should invest on, where if we need to kind of move say from batch-based system to event-based system that gives us much more scalability, which parts of which batch programs are really not scaling, which programs take how long. So as we kind of generated these metrics it kind of led to a very steady state about which things we could prioritize, that slowly we could rewrite, we could keep it in a shadow mode for a long enough time where it's just comparing the results and it's not trying to do anything in production immediately, or just using web labs or AB testing where we would slowly dial up these things, make sure it works, and then come back. And in about one and a half to two years I think we were able to rewrite a lot of the stuff without feeling the pain of big-bang launches. So interesting, yeah — technical debt is — I don't think there's a magic wand that will ever take away all the technical debt. No, it's always very step by step, very gradual, but if you keep doing it, it almost, you know, has this cascading impact where it would have created a lot of free bandwidth by fixing one small thing and then the cumulative effect is what I feel kind of lands us at a stage where we are now ready for much bigger things.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah. I see it as similar to borrowing money as an investment in a business, right? You borrow to grow and then you have to pay it back. Yeah. You mentioned — it sounded like you mentioned a trade-off between batch and event processing. Was that a call that you made, and if so, why did you make that call? What was the what drove the decision?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So I wouldn't say I made the decision. Or I mean, I identified the need for that decision either. It was much more bottom-up where one of the engineers was actually able to find out a lot of the problems we were having. And engineers face the brunt of the operations that they are supporting, right. So which is where this was actually to the engineer's credit that they brought this up. They also worked on a technical design doc that they got reviewed through a lot of very senior engineers in the organization and they went ahead and implemented it and that actually was a pretty good success. It wasn't again immediate. I would say it took several months as we did this, but it was a multi-million dollar savings in revenue — or revenue that we otherwise were losing which we hadn't realized. And it was a recovery of all those millions of dollars of recovery by just changing something from batch-based to event-based. It was a way where, you know, it wasn't just the kind of methodology from batch to event. There were a lot of other dashboards and a lot of other metrics that we generated. It was a complete overhauling of how we process certain types of agreements, which kind of led us to all these discoveries about us losing so much of revenue.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: It was more of a refactoring then.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: It was more of a refactoring. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Totally.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: That's a — trying to — I'll put this in the question. How did you handle balancing, let's say, new features or whole new products with the refactoring — which was probably happening simultaneously? How did you handle that?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So I think once we were able to establish how much revenue we are potentially losing through a non-scalable system, it wasn't that difficult of a sell to just say that this needs refactoring. So we had a monetary value attached to refactoring this. So it wasn't — it didn't look like a pure engineering project. There was a lot of interest on our business teams as well. And okay so, in that sense, it kind of exceeded the expectations in terms of how much revenue we were otherwise losing — it was like almost double-digit million dollar revenue, it's not trivial. So the decision was an easyish one. Yeah, it was. Because I think, and again, the platform that we set with trying to generate a lot of metrics kind of enabled a lot of these kind of decisions where it was easy to sell that, hey, we are in a system — if we had gone cribbing to our business saying hey our systems don't scale, probably they wouldn't be as invested in it, like it was our job to probably develop good systems. But if the cost of not having scalable systems led to what kind of problems is what I think these metrics enabled.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Great, that's thank you for that. It's interesting. Let's take another turn — a little more general question but I think for an engineering manager this is important to understand. Tell me a little bit about your leadership style.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So my leadership style is a lot more — I would say — growth and mentoring focused. So I am very invested in my team's growth, mentoring, and empowering them. And I'm generally able to grow my teams well because I also like to have a good vision for the team and kind of set ambitious goals and you know try to drive the big bets, so to say. Now that doesn't mean that I don't care for the immediate goals or the short-term goals, but generally speaking if these immediate term goals or the short-term goals can become building blocks to the long-term goals, that's how I generally try to get the set — or structure my organizations around those kind of philosophies. I think I have been pretty successful in that in terms of growing a lot of people and also getting a lot of the big things achieved.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: So — excuse me — balancing between short and long-term goals. How do you keep your team accountable to the goals that you set for them?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So in terms of accountability, I think each engineer has the kind of goals set for themselves. And then there's constant feedback that I use with them. If I see them doing something that they shouldn't, I would make sure that they get that feedback during the one-on-ones and other places. There's generally a lot of the reporting that different engineers have for the different projects they own. I kind of review that and make sure that the weekly status reports and other mechanisms that we have in play in terms of tracking projects — those are done. I also kind of am very passionate about operational excellence. I kind of always make sure that each engineer takes the responsibility of making sure that they have enough goals around operational excellence and they are kind of continuously improving our systems. They are not just doing the business projects and all that productivity — whether it's in terms of how many lines of code they shipped or how much code review and code review effectiveness they have had — those are the things I track, and you know if there is feedback needed I make sure that I don't wait for the end of the year to give them this feedback. I make sure they get it as and when things happen so that they have a chance to course correct.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, sounds good. That sounds good. What drew you to engineering management? From my own experience, some engineers never want to touch management and want to be ICs or independent contributors, and some move into the EM track. What brought you to the EM track?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I think I was a reluctant entry to engineering management. I would say it took me some time to like managing people. My first few — I would say at least months — was more about — because I was very good as an IC. I just thought that that would be a better way to just help more people and kind of impart that knowledge and skills that I had gained as an IC to be able to kind of spread to a much bigger team much more easily. That's how I started it. But then I also learned over a period of time that engineering management is not like just technical knowledge. There's a lot more to it. And some of those I learned over a period of time. I don't think I learned it on day one or I knew what I was getting into on day one. But I started enjoying it. There was a time when I also started managing managers. But the more important thing was always about — I still had that fundamental desire that I should grow people. And that's kind of always kept me much more motivated.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: That's nice to hear. Yeah, it's a — I'm sure it was a learning process to become the engineering manager you are. So, this takes me I think to our last question. Looking to the future, what are you looking for in your next role?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So from the next role I think it's being able to solve complex and ambiguous problems and along the way, you know, mentor and empower my teams to solve these kinds of problems and lend that experience of people management and having solved a lot of complex ambiguous problems — just be able to use those skills and experience to grow more people. I would say.

training_pairs.question: Why don't you go ahead and tell me about yourself?
training_pairs.answer: Hey Josh, nice to meet you. Thanks for having me. So yeah, I'm Ragu. I'm an engineering manager. I spent over 10 years as an engineering manager with big large companies. I have had the opportunity to, you know, start and like be a founder member for large multiple engineering teams. I've grown global teams across London and India and yeah I've been very successful at hiring and growing teams and landing a lot of impact through delivering large bets for the organizations I worked with.
training_pairs.follow_up: Tell me about a time that you resolved a conflict.

training_pairs.question: Tell me about a time that you resolved a conflict.
training_pairs.answer: Okay. So this was a time when we were doing this project at Amazon where entire Amazon was actually involved in something called RedShift, which is basically deprecating Oracle databases and moving to Amazon's internal tech stack using either DynamoDB or the relational database service. And obviously all teams were frantically trying to get to their goals of being able to do this. Now, one of the things which happened at the time was we realized we had to make changes to one of our services. We almost had to rewrite it because the way it was implemented was, if it was an Oracle database it was allowed to store some of the PII data, but if we have to move out we would have had to build another solution. Now the main conflict there was that we realized very quickly that we shouldn't even be storing that — we were not the source of truth for that data and that data should reside in some other system. Now obviously when we approached that team that owned that data they were challenged with their own timelines and they did not have the bandwidth to make these changes. So we had a couple of meetings and we did not end up with any solution. So one of the ways which I thought at the time is that the engineer who owned this project from my team was on a career path to the next level and needed an opportunity to kind of showcase or demonstrate those next level behaviors. So I kind of created a plan where we would do some of the initial work which would just set a platform for moving this data into the upstream team, and we will just store the metadata about that particular data and create service endpoints for them to use later. We next went to them with this approach. They still would have had to do a little bit of work. It wasn't going to be much for them. The engineer did the work, it was a bit of a win-win for everyone. The manager of the other team decided to give good feedback for the engineer on my team who got promoted and we all kind of were able to complete the project on time.
training_pairs.follow_up: That sounds like that was a difficult or challenging situation. What was your specific role in this situation?

training_pairs.question: What was your specific role in this situation?
training_pairs.answer: My specific role was working with the engineer from my team to come up with a solution for this problem. We had to make sure that we could not complete this project without moving out some of the PII data and if it had to go in and sit in some other team, the logical owners for that data did not have the bandwidth. So I had to kind of mediate with the engineers and the engineering manager of that team along with the engineer on my team and come up with a solution that works for everyone. And then once we cross this obstacle and go live with the RedShift project, we would then work with the upstream team again to make sure we have a different milestone where we migrate off that service completely and they are able to kind of stand up their service which would own the data.
training_pairs.follow_up: What would you say was the impact of this project? Or maybe better said, what would happen if nothing were done?

training_pairs.question: What would you say was the impact of this project? What would happen if nothing were done — if you hadn't led through this?
training_pairs.answer: I don't think that was an option. The other options were: stay with Oracle, which was not an option; continue storing the PII data, which was again not an option because DynamoDB does not allow — or at least back then did not allow — us to store that data securely; and the upstream team would have to build and launch a new service which was the right thing to do but we kind of just helped them to come up with a solution in the intermediate that would work, where we stored the metadata. We temporarily created a service that would store the data on their side and we were the only client for that period and then they would kind of expand it to other clients.
training_pairs.follow_up: So what was the impact to the business by achieving this project?

training_pairs.question: So what was the impact to the business by achieving this project?
training_pairs.answer: So the RedShift project was a project that came straight from the S-team at Amazon, which is like the very top senior leadership at Amazon. So it was again not negotiable in any way, either in terms of the timelines or the solution. So if we hadn't done this, there was no material benefit to any of the business teams across Amazon for people doing this project, but it had a much bigger impact with the fact that we would be saving probably hundreds of millions of dollars by using our own technology. So cost savings by using Amazon technology for storing this sensitive data.
training_pairs.follow_up: What would you say is a product that you're most proud of?

training_pairs.question: What would you say is a product that you're most proud of?
training_pairs.answer: So I can tell about an accounting close platform that I developed. I was one of the first, and I'm proud of it for multiple reasons. A: this was a ground-up new product which became the central tool of choice for the company I was working in to complete all the financial close activity for that company. B: this was not only a ground-up new product. This was also a team that I developed — basically hired and grew as part of this team that I started. So I started a new team. I hired a lot of good engineers who went on to do very successful things for the product. I was also able to deprecate a lot of legacy systems. I was able to stand up this tool to become the single unified tool for performing financial flows automation.
training_pairs.follow_up: What would you say was the highlight of that project for you? What made you the most proud?

training_pairs.question: What would you say was the highlight of that project for you? What made you the most proud?
training_pairs.answer: The fact that it was a big platform. There were four different modules in it and it was a huge product and at the same time it was very stable from its very go. We had only maybe 40 or 50 people who used the product at launch. It went on to have like more than 500 users within a year or two. And at the same time it was one of those rare products that never got any — you know what we call severe incidents or high priority tasks that kind of blocked users. It was extremely stable in terms of quality. And that kind of led to a lot of adoption of the tool and we kept on adding a lot of big features and yet it always seemed to work scalably without going down or having any kind of product issues.
training_pairs.follow_up: Tell me about a decision or a trade-off you had to make in that project.

training_pairs.question: Tell me about a decision or a trade-off you had to make in that project.
training_pairs.answer: I think one of the decisions was, once we started getting a lot of adoption, what I realized is one of the modules had a backend engine which we realized is not going to scale. Now we had an option to go back to the users and say we need to kind of rebuild the internals of that engine to kind of make sure it scales for all the quick adoption that's happening — from like a few hundred cards we would be going to thousands of cards in less than six months. So one of the decisions I took was we stopped a new feature that we were developing to re-prioritize rebuilding this engine. And we did it in a way where we did not want the users to come back and do all these long UATs. We created a service that would compare the before and after in a very easy way for engineers to kind of just work with it. And we kind of created reporting that would go out and show that the new code did exactly what the old code would have done except that it is now far more scalable. So with that what happened is we were able to scale for the year end because the year end close is almost always the most critical for a company. And we went live with this rewriting of the engine and it scaled and we did not have any other problem. Now we did not have a new feature which came later in Q1 but that feature was not — would not have soured the experience of year end close as such.
training_pairs.follow_up: How do you view technical debt generally, and can you tell me about a time you had to work with it?

training_pairs.question: How do you view technical debt generally speaking? And tell me about a time where you had to work with some technical debt.
training_pairs.answer: Sure. So I think I just gave an example where I think we kind of built a system that did not have technical debt, or which kind of did not ever lead to a situation with tech debt. So that's the highlight of my career in terms of building that kind of a product. But I would also say that that's because I had a lot of experience getting hurt with technical debt in the past. So there were a lot of lessons learned on, you know, what not to do, which I think I was successful in this new project. But going back to your question, Josh — so I had teams that I have kind of joined, or products that I have inherited, that had a lot of tech debt. And my observation is we always tend to either talk too much into the future — if we need to kind of fix this we probably need to rebuild the whole thing, or we need to kind of have a two-year engineering plan to kind of rewrite the product or something like that. But I can give some example where one of the teams that I was leading did have a lot of tech debt because it was written years ago when the team was much smaller, the product was much smaller, it wasn't processing the kind of billions of dollars that it was processing at that time. So there were a few tiny steps that I think I had my team take on a continuous basis. Just improve the overall instrumentation in the code and start gathering metrics — do we even know what's working well and what's not? And if something is not working well, can we get more insights on a week-to-week basis. So one or two of the sprints I had my team purely work on adding more instrumentation and generating the metrics that would go into our alerting and monitoring tool. But one of the things we started seeing is there were a lot of misconceptions we had about our own product. We used to have a website and we thought that's where everyone comes and creates these agreements. But what we realized is more than 70% of the agreements in our system are created by an upstream team calling our APIs — never through the website. So editing an agreement is what was done mostly on our website, never much of creation. So we built all this kind of metrics continuously and it led to something much more impactful. It helped us to identify where we should focus, what are the things that we should invest on, where if we need to move from batch-based system to event-based system, which parts of which batch programs are really not scaling, which programs take how long. So as we kind of generated these metrics it kind of led to a very steady state about which things we could prioritize, that slowly we could rewrite, we could keep it in a shadow mode for a long enough time where it's just comparing the results. And in about one and a half to two years I think we were able to rewrite a lot of the stuff without feeling the pain of big-bang launches. Technical debt — I don't think there's a magic wand that will ever take away all the technical debt. It's always very step by step, very gradual, but if you keep doing it it has this cascading impact where it would have created a lot of free bandwidth by fixing one small thing and the cumulative effect is what I feel kind of lands us at a stage where we are now ready for much bigger things.
training_pairs.follow_up: You mentioned a trade-off between batch and event processing. Was that a call that you made, and if so why did you make that call?

training_pairs.question: You mentioned a trade-off between batch and event processing. Was that a call that you made, and if so why did you make that call? What drove the decision?
training_pairs.answer: So I wouldn't say I made the decision. Or I mean, I identified the need for that decision either. It was much more bottom-up where one of the engineers was actually able to find out a lot of the problems we were having. And engineers face the brunt of the operations that they are supporting, right. So this was actually to the engineer's credit that they brought this up. They also worked on a technical design doc that they got reviewed through a lot of very senior engineers in the organization and they went ahead and implemented it and that actually was a pretty good success. It wasn't again immediate. I would say it took several months as we did this, but it was a multi-million dollar savings in revenue — or revenue that we otherwise were losing which we hadn't realized. And it was a recovery of all those millions of dollars by just changing something from batch-based to event-based. It was a way where, you know, it wasn't just the kind of methodology from batch to event. There were a lot of other dashboards and a lot of other metrics that we generated. It was a complete overhauling of how we process certain types of agreements, which kind of led us to all these discoveries about us losing so much of revenue.
training_pairs.follow_up: How did you handle balancing new features or whole new products with the refactoring that was probably happening simultaneously?

training_pairs.question: How did you handle balancing new features or whole new products with the refactoring happening simultaneously?
training_pairs.answer: So I think once we were able to establish how much revenue we are potentially losing through a non-scalable system, it wasn't that difficult of a sell to just say that this needs refactoring. So we had a monetary value attached to refactoring this. So it didn't look like a pure engineering project. There was a lot of interest on our business teams as well. And it kind of exceeded the expectations in terms of how much revenue we were otherwise losing — it was like almost double-digit million dollar revenue, it's not trivial. So the decision was an easyish one. Because the platform that we set with trying to generate a lot of metrics kind of enabled a lot of these kind of decisions where it was easy to sell that, hey — if we had gone cribbing to our business saying hey our systems don't scale, probably they wouldn't be as invested in it. But the cost of not having scalable systems, and what kind of problems that led to, is what these metrics enabled.
training_pairs.follow_up: Tell me a little bit about your leadership style.

training_pairs.question: Tell me a little bit about your leadership style.
training_pairs.answer: So my leadership style is a lot more — I would say — growth and mentoring focused. So I am very invested in my team's growth, mentoring, and empowering them. And I'm generally able to grow my teams well because I also like to have a good vision for the team and kind of set ambitious goals and you know try to drive the big bets, so to say. Now that doesn't mean that I don't care for the immediate goals or the short-term goals, but generally speaking if these immediate term goals or the short-term goals can become building blocks to the long-term goals, that's how I generally try to structure my organizations around those kind of philosophies. I think I have been pretty successful in that in terms of growing a lot of people and also getting a lot of the big things achieved.
training_pairs.follow_up: How do you keep your team accountable to the goals that you set for them?

training_pairs.question: How do you keep your team accountable to the goals that you set for them?
training_pairs.answer: So in terms of accountability, I think each engineer has the kind of goals set for themselves. And then there's constant feedback that I use with them. If I see them doing something that they shouldn't, I would make sure that they get that feedback during the one-on-ones and other places. There's generally a lot of the reporting that different engineers have for the different projects they own. I kind of review that and make sure that the weekly status reports and other mechanisms that we have in play in terms of tracking projects — those are done. I also kind of am very passionate about operational excellence. I kind of always make sure that each engineer takes the responsibility of making sure that they have enough goals around operational excellence and they are kind of continuously improving our systems. They are not just doing the business projects and all that productivity — whether it's in terms of how many lines of code they shipped or how much code review and code review effectiveness they have had — those are the things I track, and if there is feedback needed I make sure that I don't wait for the end of the year to give them this feedback. I make sure they get it as and when things happen so that they have a chance to course correct.
training_pairs.follow_up: What drew you to engineering management?

training_pairs.question: What drew you to engineering management?
training_pairs.answer: I think I was a reluctant entry to engineering management. I would say it took me some time to like managing people. My first few — I would say at least months — was more about — because I was very good as an IC. I just thought that that would be a better way to just help more people and kind of impart that knowledge and skills that I had gained as an IC to be able to kind of spread to a much bigger team much more easily. That's how I started it. But then I also learned over a period of time that engineering management is not like just technical knowledge. There's a lot more to it. And some of those I learned over a period of time. I don't think I learned it on day one or I knew what I was getting into on day one. But I started enjoying it. There was a time when I also started managing managers. But the more important thing was always about — I still had that fundamental desire that I should grow people. And that's kind of always kept me much more motivated.
training_pairs.follow_up: Looking to the future, what are you looking for in your next role?

training_pairs.question: Looking to the future, what are you looking for in your next role?
training_pairs.answer: So from the next role I think it's being able to solve complex and ambiguous problems and along the way, you know, mentor and empower my teams to solve these kinds of problems and lend that experience of people management and having solved a lot of complex ambiguous problems — just be able to use those skills and experience to grow more people. I would say.
